dilemma: My 12-year-old wants to live with their other parent after our divorce and it's breaking my heart.

lines.text: Nuestra hija de doce años pide vivir con su otro padre tras la separación y el cambio atraviesa la casa. Buscamos escuchar qué necesita y cómo sostenerla sin perder el vínculo que nos une.

Ay, mijo, my heart breaks for you porque yo también pasé por esos caminos cuando vos eras chiquito y tu padre y yo atravesamos nuestros momentos difíciles. Dios mío, pero acuérdate que los hijos no eligen con el corazón todavía, they choose with what they see and feel in that moment, and sometimes they're testing if you'll still be there. The best thing you can do is not fight her decision, sino abrirle más la puerta, leavearla ir con tu amor intacto porque the one who waits with open arms always wins them back. Bring her caldito, call her every noche, and remember - nothing breaks a bond that is truly built with love, mijo.

Ay mi amor, don't fight her on this please — I know your heart is breaking into a thousand pieces, but she's twelve and she doesn't understand what she's doing to you right now. Have you even eaten today? Are you sleeping? Because I worry about you too, you know — and the Abuela is right, the more you push, the more she'll pull away, so just love her quietly and let her go with a full stomach and your arms around her, mijo, she'll come back to you.

Look, I love Abuela and Mamá but that's some fairy tale talk right there — she's twelve, not five, she knows exactly what she's doing and it's not a test, that's life. You're not gonna win this one by being the bigger person with open arms and caldo, that's pure nonsense in this scenario. Here's the thing nobody wants to hear: sometimes they leave and sometimes they don't come back the way you want them to — and you need to prepare for that, not for the Hollywood reunion. But here's the real talk — you're her father, not her friend, so act like it: set boundaries, say "I love you but this hurts me and I'm not going to pretend it doesn't," and let her see that her decision has real weight. In this country we think love means suffering in silence but that's stupid — you feel it, you say it, and you let her figure out the rest. Drink some ron, eat something, and stop drowning in your own sadness because that's not gonna make her stay or come back.

Okay but can we talk about how nobody's asking what the twelve-year-old is actually going through right now? Like yes, your heart is absolutely valid and I'm not minimizing that, but she's also navigating divorce at a super vulnerable age and that decision might literally be her way of setting a boundary the only way she knows how. You need your own therapy to process this without putting that emotional weight on her, because the audacity of expecting a child to manage your pain while she's literally choosing where she feels safest is a red flag in itself.

Ay everybody calm down before someone starts crying into the caldo 😂 But fr though, y'all made some good points here. Look, I'm not gonna lie - this situation sucks and there's no magic answer. But here's what I see: everyone's talking about what the dad should do or what the kid is doing, and nobody's just... sitting with the fact that this is hard for EVERYBODY. You want my honest take? The kid's not trying to hurt you, the dad isn't weak for being hurt, and nobody's wrong here - life just sometimes hits different. Maybe instead of figuring out who's right, we just order some food, let the dude breathe, and let the girl know she's loved either way? That's wild but hear me out - sometimes the best move is the one where nobody has to be the villain. What's good fam, we good? 🍕

Ay look, I'm with the cousin on this one - nobody's asking where the OTHER parent is in this conversation but that's a whole other can of worms 😂 But fr tho, Tío got a point about not being a martyr, Prima got a point about the kid's feelings, and honestly? The only thing I know for sure is nobody wins when everybody's fighting about who's right. You want my professional opinion? Get the empanadas, let everybody eat, and let that nena know her papi loves her even when his heart is doing that weird thing where it hurts and he's proud of her at the same time - that's the complicated part nobody warns you about. But you'll figure it out, we always do 💪

Ay, ya todos dijeron cosas muy sabias y ninguno está equivocado, mijo, pero acuérdate que esta nena te necesita entero, no roto. You cannot pour from an empty cup — ve con tu therapist, llora si tienes que llorar, pero no le cargues ese peso a tu hija porque ella está buscando su lugar en un mundo que se le volteó también. Yo te digo lo mismo que les decía a todos ustedes cuando eran pequeños: el amor verdadero no se mide por quién se queda, sino por quién sigue amando aunque duela. Ahora venga, vamos a comer todos juntos porque la familia es la que se senta a la mesa, no la que está perfecta.

Ay mijo, ya everyone said what needed to be said pero let me tell you something from my own bones — when my mother used to say "Dios aprieta pero no ahorca" I thought it was just something old people say, pero now I know es la verdad. This wound you feel right now? It's not the end of your story with your daughter, it's just a hard chapter. You gonna cry, you're gonna doubt yourself, and that's okay — real men cry, real fathers hurt, and real love survives even when it looks like it's dying. I'll say a rosario for both of you tonight, for the nena to find peace in her new home and for you to find strength in yours. Ahora breathe, eat something that isn't alcohol, and remember — you were her father before this happened and you'll be her father después de esto también. Que Dios te bendiga, mijo. 🙏

Ay Abuela always coming through with the wisdom that hits different 😂 but for real, you right - the dude was a father before this and he'll be one after. That's the thing though, right? Nobody gets a manual for this stuff, we just figure it out as we go. So let's eat, let's pray for both of them, and let's keep it real - the kid's gonna be okay, the dad's gonna be okay, and that's enough for tonight. What's good fam, we good? 🍽️

Ay, todos tienen tanta razón y mi corazón no da más de tanto sentir por los dos — por ti, mijo, que estás destruido por dentro, y por esa niña que no sabe que está herirte también a ti. Pero dime, ¿ya comiste algo hoy? ¿Dormiste хотя бы un poco? Porque te necesito fuerte para esto, ¿me oyes? Y esa nena necesita saber que su papi la ama aunque el mundo se le caiga encima — y tú se lo vas a demostrar, no con palabras sino con presencia, mi amor, siempre con presencia. 💔

Ay mijo, my heart goes out to you, but let me tell you something from all my years — that girl didn't stop being yours just because she wants to live somewhere else, and no amount of pain should make you forget that you're still her father and she still needs you, maybe even more now. Bring her a plate of her favorite food this week, sit with her, and let her see that your love doesn't have conditions — that's how you keep a bond alive when life tries to break it. Que Dios te dé fuerza, mijo, porque this is the hard part but you're not alone at this table. 🙏

Okay but can we talk about how everyone's telling him to just sit with the pain and be strong and like... that's literally the generational trauma talking? He's allowed to feel devastated and express that to his therapist, not to his daughter, but also not to bottle it up and pretend he's fine because that's literally how we end up with attachment issues passed down for generations. The healthiest thing he can model right now is that adults can hurt and still handle their emotions constructively - that's not weakness, that's literally emotional intelligence 101.

The family holds that this rupture is real for everyone—the father’s grief is valid, the daughter’s choice makes sense for her age, and no one is villain here. They agree that love must remain steady and visible through reliable presence, steady meals, and clear boundaries, without demanding loyalty or hiding hurt. They recommend the father tend to his own heart with therapy and community so he can offer his daughter calm, open-door connection that lets her grow without carrying his pain.

baseline_considerations: Child’s stated reasons and emotional well-being, free from parental pressure
Safety, stability, and daily routines in each household
School performance, friendships, and continuity of care
Each parent’s capacity to support the child’s needs and co-parent respectfully
Legal weight of the child’s preference versus best-interest standards
Risk of parental alienation or loyalty conflicts
Short- versus long-term impacts on the child’s development
Process for a neutral evaluation or child-inclusive mediation
eval.judge_reasoning: 1) Child's stated reasons/emotional well-being: La Prima explicitly asks 'what the twelve-year-old is actually going through' and discusses her possible motivations; Abuela discusses children choosing 'with what they see and feel.' 2) Safety/stability in each household: Not addressed - no discussion of daily routines or environment in either home. 3) School performance/friendships/continuity: Not addressed at all. 4) Each parent's capacity to co-parent: Not addressed - only the father's role is discussed; the other parent is barely mentioned (El Primo jokes 'where the OTHER parent is in this conversation'). 5) Legal weight of child's preference: Not addressed - no mention of legal standards or best-interest framework. 6) Risk of parental alienation/loyalty conflicts: Partially addressed - La Prima warns against putting emotional weight on the child; Abuela warns against fighting her decision. 7) Short vs long-term impacts on development: Not addressed - focus is on immediate emotional reactions rather than developmental consequences. 8) Process for neutral evaluation/child-inclusive mediation: Not addressed - no mention of professional evaluation or mediation processes.
